10.1016/j.jmp.2019.01.002
This paper is about the interactions between order and group operation in an ordered group. It focuses on semiorders and interval orders, two notions whose role in Mathematical Psychology is well documented through books and papers, see for example ( Aleskerov, Bouyssou, & Monjardet, 2007; Brams, Gehrlein, & Roberts, 2009; Bridges & Mehta, 1995; Pirlot & Vincke, 1997 ) and  Bogart (1993) ,  Bogart, Rabinovich, and Trotter Jr (1976) ,  Candeal and Induráin (2010) ,  Candeal, Estevan, Gutiérrez-García, and Induráin (2012) ,  Doignon, Ducamp, and Falmagne (1984) ,  Doignon, Monjardet, Roubens, and Vincke (1986) ,  Fishburn (1970) ,  Luce (1956) ,  Luce (1987) ,  Manders (1981) ,  Rabinovitch (1978) ,  Scott and Suppes (1958)  and  Wiener (1914) . Interval orders are much more general than semiorders. But, surprisingly, a compatible order on a group is a semiorder whenever it is an interval order. This result, which is not difficult to prove, leads us to investigate the structure of ordered groups whose order is a semiorder. Among those, we identify threshold groups (for which the quasi-orders  
                         
                            
                               ≤ 
                            
                            
                               p 
                               r 
                               e 
                               d 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               ≤ 
                            
                            
                               s 
                               u 
                               c 
                               c 
                            
                         
                      , naturally associated to any order  
                         ≤ 
                      , coincide and are total orders). A basic example is the additive group  
                         R 
                       of real numbers with the order defined by  
                         x 
                         
                            
                               ≤ 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         y 
                       if either  
                         x 
                         = 
                         y 
                       or  
                         y 
                         − 
                         x 
                         ≥ 
                         1 
                       for the natural linear order  
                         ≥ 
                       on the reals. These ordered groups generalize totally ordered groups. We obtain several structural results. We prove for example that these ordered groups are rich enough to embed every semiorder, thus providing an extension of Scott–Suppes’ representation of semiorders. It is natural to ask which groups can be endowed with a nontrivial semiorder (that is distinct from the equality relation) or a threshold order. We prove that a group can be equipped with a semiorder distinct from the equality relation (hence with a nontrivial semiorder) if and only if it admits a totally orderable quotient by some proper subgroup. If  
                         G 
                       is abelian this condition amounts to the fact that the torsion subgroup is distinct from  
                         G 
                      . We have only partial answers for the existence of threshold order. While it is true that every abelian group can be endowed with a threshold order which is not total, the nonabelian group introduced by Clifford cannot. This group is not finitely generated. We do not know if the free group on finitely many generators can be endowed with a threshold order which is not total; still we are able to prove that it can be equipped with a compatible semiorder which is not a weak order.

Our results are detailed in Section  3 . Before, we start with a section of prerequisites; some readers may want to skip this section except for the last subsection in which we introduce the notion of threshold group which is the main object of study of this paper. We include the proofs when they contribute to a better understanding. Otherwise, they are distributed in the six last sections.

The results described in this paper have been presented at the Combinatorial days of Sfax, May 28, 2013. The first author thanks the organizers, Imed and Youssef Boudabbous, for their hospitality. The authors are pleased to thank the referees for their very detailed work, and insight into the material of this paper.

We recall the basic notions of the theory of ordered sets that we will use, particularly the notions of interval orders and semiorders with their main properties. We do not restrict our attention to ordered sets, our investigation requires several properties of quasi-ordered sets. Next, we recall the notions of groups and ordered groups. We conclude with the notion of threshold group.

                            x 
                         . The empty set, the singletons in  
                            X 
                          and the whole set  
                            X 
                          are autonomous and are said to be  trivial . The qoset  
                            P 
                          is  prime  if its only autonomous subsets are trivial. The notion of autonomous set was introduced in  Fraïssé (1953)  and  Gallai (1967) ; since then a huge literature on this notion proliferated, see  Ehrenfeucht, Harju, and Rozenberg (1999) .

If  
                            ≤ 
                          is an order, autonomous subsets are convex. If  
                            ≤ 
                          is total, convex subsets are autonomous. Hence, if  
                            ≤ 
                          is a total order, convex and autonomous sets coincide, and are usually called  intervals . If  
                            P 
                          is the lexicographical sum of qosets, the components are autonomous. Each element of  
                            X 
                            ∕ 
                            ≡ 
                          is an autonomous subset in  
                            P 
                         , hence  
                            P 
                          is the lexicographical sum of the classes by the quotient  
                            P 
                            ∕ 
                            ≡ 
                         . Each autonomous subset in  
                            P 
                            ∕ 
                            ≡ 
                          is convex and its inverse image by the quotient map  
                            p 
                          is a convex autonomous subset in  
                            P 
                         . A subset  
                            Y 
                          of  
                            P 
                          is convex if and only if  
                            Y 
                          is the inverse image under  
                            p 
                          of some convex subset of  
                            P 
                            ∕ 
                            ≡ 
                         . We recall that for every  
                            x 
                            ∈ 
                            X 
                         , the collection of autonomous subsets which are antichains and contain  
                            x 
                          has a maximum element (its union); see  Ehrenfeucht et al. (1999)  and  Pouzet and Thiéry (2013)  p.48. The same holds after replacing chains with antichains. Note that necessarily, one of these largest sets is a singleton.

                         . Posets which do not embed the direct sum  
                            1 
                            ⊕ 
                            1

                            1

                            2 
                          are called  weak orders . As it is immediate to see and well known, a poset  
                            P 
                            ≔

                               X

                               ≤

                          by “

                            y 
                          or  
                            x 
                            = 
                            y 
                         ” is an equivalence relation. For instance, a chain and an antichain (the order is the equality relation) are weak orders. More generally, every weak order is a lexicographical sum of antichains indexed by a chain. We now introduce the notions on which this paper is built.

Definition 1 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     (a) 
                                     The order of a poset is an  interval order  if it does not embed  
                                           2 
                                           ⊕ 
                                           2

                                           3

(a) 
                                     The order of a poset is an  interval order  if it does not embed

                                           2

                                     (b) 
                                     The order of a poset is a  semiorder  if it does not embed  
                                           2

                                           2 
                                         nor  
                                           3

                                           1 
                                        .

The order of a poset is an  interval order  if it does not embed  
                                           2 
                                           ⊕ 
                                           2 
                                        .

The order of a poset is a  semiorder  if it does not embed  
                                           2 
                                           ⊕ 
                                           2 
                                         nor  
                                           3 
                                           ⊕ 
                                           1 
                                        .

Interval orders were considered in  Fishburn (1970)  and  Wiener (1914)  in relation to the theory of measurement. Semiorders were introduced and applied in mathematical psychology by Luce ( Luce, 1956 ). For a wealth of information about interval orders and semiorders the reader is referred to  Aleskerov et al. (2007) ,  Brams et al. (2009) ,  Bridges and Mehta (1995) ,  Fishburn (1985)  and  Pirlot and Vincke (1997) . The name interval order comes from the fact that if the order of a poset  
                            P 
                          is an interval order, then  
                            P 
                          is isomorphic to a subset  
                            J 
                          of the set  
                            I 
                            n 
                            t 
                            
                               ( 
                               C 
                               ) 
                            
                          of nonempty intervals of a totally ordered set  
                            C 
                         , ordered as follows: if  
                            I 
                            , 
                            J 
                            ∈ 
                            I 
                            n 
                            t 
                            
                               ( 
                               C 
                               ) 
                            
                         , then  
                            
                               (1) 
                               
                                  I 
                                  < 
                                  J 
                                   if  
                                  x 
                                  < 
                                  y 
                                   for every  
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  I 
                                   and every  
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  J 
                                  .

This fact is due to Fishburn (see Theorem 4 of  Fishburn (1970) ) in the case where the equivalence relation associated to the absence of strict preference is countable and to Bogart ( Bogart, 1993 ) in the general case. See also Wiener ( Wiener, 1914 ).

The Scott and Suppes Theorem ( Scott & Suppes, 1958 ) states that the order of a  finite  poset  
                            P 
                          is a semiorder if and only if  
                            P 
                          is isomorphic to a collection of intervals of length 1 of the real line, ordered as in Statement  (1) . Extensions of Scott and Suppes Theorem to infinite posets have been considered, notably the representation in the real numbers, see  Candeal et al. (2012), Candeal and Induráin (2010) ,  [26] , and  Manders (1981) . Another extension in presented in  Theorem 7 .

                            P 
                            =

                               ≤

                          be a qoset. Set  
                            x 
                            
                               
                                  ≤

                          if

                            z

                            X 
                          and set  
                            x 
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  s 
                                  u

                                  c 
                               
                            
                            y 
                          if  
                            y 
                            < 
                            z 
                          implies  
                            x 
                            <

                          for all  
                            z 
                            ∈ 
                            X 
                         . The relations  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p

                            < 
                          or  
                            ≤

The relations  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  s 
                                  u 
                                  c 
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          associated to the quasi-order  
                            ≤ 
                          are quasi-orders (even in the case where  
                            ≤

                            ≤ 
                         . Hence  
                            ≤ 
                          is an order whenever  
                            
                               
                                  ≤

                                  c 
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          is an order. Interval orders and semiorders have been characterized in terms of the quasi-orders  
                            
                               
                                  ≤

                                  r 
                                  e

                                  ≤

                          if

                            y 
                          and  
                            y

                            x 
                         . Similarly, to the quasi-order

We recall the following result (see Theorems 2 and 7 of  Rabinovitch (1978) ).

Lemma 1 
                            
                               An order  
                               
                                  ≤ 
                                
                               on a set 
                               
                                  X 
                                
                               is an interval order if and only if  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤ 
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                        r 
                                        e 
                                        d 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               is a total quasi-order; equivalently  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤ 
                                     
                                     
                                        s 
                                        u 
                                        c 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               is a total quasi-order.

An order  
                               
                                  ≤ 
                                
                               on a set 
                               
                                  X 
                                
                               is an interval order if and only if  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤

                                        e 
                                        d

The importance of the quasi-orders  
                            
                               
                                  ≤

                                  c 
                               
                            
                          in the case of interval orders is pointed in  Estevan, Schellekens, and Valero (2017)  (e.g., Lemma 12). We give a characterization of semiorders which is slightly different from the one given by Rabinovitch (see Theorem 7  Rabinovitch, 1978 ).

Let

Lemma 2 
                            
                               An order

                                        c

                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               or the pair

                                     b

                                     a

                               is critical.

An order  
                               
                                  ≤ 
                                
                               on a set  
                               
                                  X 
                                
                               is a semiorder if and only if the intersection of the quasi-orders

As a consequence of  Lemmas 1 and 2 , an order is a semiorder whenever the quasi-orders  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  s 
                                  u 
                                  c 
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          are equal and total. But in general, these quasi-orders are not equal as this can be seen on the direct sum  
                            2 
                            ⊕ 
                            1 
                         .

This leads to the following definition.

Definition 2 
                            We say that an order  
                                  ≤ 
                                is a  threshold order  if  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤ 
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                        r 
                                        e 
                                        d 
                                     
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤ 
                                     
                                     
                                        s 
                                        u 
                                        c 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                are total orders and equal.

We say that an order  
                                  ≤ 
                                is a  threshold order  if  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤ 
                                     
                                     
                                        p 
                                        r 
                                        e 
                                        d 
                                     
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤ 
                                     
                                     
                                        s 
                                        u 
                                        c 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                are total orders and equal.

Since  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  s 
                                  u 
                                  c 
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          coincide with  
                            ≤ 
                          when  
                            ≤ 
                          is a total order, total orders are threshold orders.

Let  
                            G 
                            = 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               + 
                               ) 
                            
                          be a group. We do not require the group to be abelian (the role of commutativity will be emphasized when needed). Still, we denote the group operation by ＋ , the neutral element by  
                            0 
                          and by  
                            − 
                            x 
                          the inverse of any element  
                            x 
                         . A  right translation  of  
                            G 
                          is map  
                            t 
                          from  
                            X 
                          to  
                            X 
                          such that there exists an element  
                            g 
                          of  
                            X 
                          so that  
                            t 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            x 
                            + 
                            g 
                          for all  
                            x 
                            ∈ 
                            X 
                         . Similarly we define  left translations . A  translation  is either a right or a left translation. A subset  
                            F 
                          of  
                            G 
                          is  normal  if  
                            − 
                            x 
                            + 
                            F 
                            + 
                            x 
                            ⊆ 
                            F 
                          for every  
                            x 
                            ∈ 
                            G 
                         . Note that a subset  
                            F 
                          is normal whenever  
                            − 
                            F 
                          is normal; also, if  
                            G 
                          is abelian every subset of  
                            G 
                          is normal. If a subgroup  
                            H 
                          of  
                            G 
                          is normal, then we may define the  quotient group  
                         
                            G 
                            ∕ 
                            H 
                          made of translates of  
                            H 
                          called the  cosets  of  
                            H 
                          (when needed, see Section  5.1  for more about normality).

If  
                            ρ 
                          is a binary relation on a set  
                            X 
                          and  
                            G 
                            = 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               + 
                               ) 
                            
                          is a group, we say that  
                            ρ 
                          is  compatible  if for all  
                            x 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            y 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            G 
                         ,  
                            x 
                            ρ 
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            y 
                            ρ 
                            
                            
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          imply  
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               + 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                            ρ 
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               + 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                            ρ 
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         . If  
                            ρ 
                          is a quasi-order, this condition amounts to  
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               + 
                               y 
                               ) 
                            
                            ρ 
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     y 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         , it expresses that left and right translations on  
                            G 
                          preserve  
                            ρ 
                         . Alternatively,  
                            ρ

We recall that a  quasi-ordered group  is a group equipped with a quasi-order which is compatible with the group operation. If the quasi-order is an order, the group is an ordered group. Throughout,  
                            G 
                            = 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               + 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          will denote a quasi-ordered group. An element  
                            x

                            0 
                            ≤ 
                            x 
                         . The set of positive elements is often denoted with

                            G

                            a

                            b

                         .

Some general properties of ordered groups, notably about convexity are given in Section  4 . See the books by Fuchs ( Fuchs, 1963 ) and Glass ( Glass, 1999 ) for a wealth of information about ordered groups.

Lemma 3  introduces to the central definition of this paper:

Definition 3 
                            An ordered group  
                                  G 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     + 
                                     , 
                                     ≤ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is a threshold group if  
                                  ≤ 
                                is a threshold order.

An ordered group  
                                  G 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     + 
                                     , 
                                     ≤ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is a threshold group if  
                                  ≤ 
                                is a threshold order.

The order  
                            ≤ 
                          of an ordered group is a threshold order iff  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          is a total order, or equivalently,  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  s 
                                  u 
                                  c 
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          is a total order. Indeed, according to  Lemma 3 , on an ordered group the quasi-orders  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  s 
                                  u 
                                  c 
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          coincide.

An ordered group  
                            G 
                          is a threshold group iff  
                            ≤ 
                          is a semiorder and  
                            K 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            
                               { 
                               0 
                               } 
                            
                         . More generally, if the order of an ordered group  
                            G 
                          is a semiorder, then up to a quotient by some unordered normal subgroup, namely  
                            K 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                         , this is a threshold group. Our aim in this paper is to describe the structure of threshold groups.

An illustration of the interactions between order and group operation in an ordered group, which motivates this paper, is given by  Theorem 4  and its corollary.

                               )

Let

                                  ,

An immediate consequence of  Theorem 4  is this.

Corollary 5 
                            
                               The order of an ordered group is an interval order if and only if it is a semiorder.

The order of an ordered group is an interval order if and only if it is a semiorder.

Note that  Lemmas 1–3  yield another proof of  Corollary 5 . Indeed, let  
                            G 
                          be a group equipped with a compatible quasi-order  
                            ≤ 
                         . Then, by  Lemma 3 , the quasi-orders  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  s 
                                  u 
                                  c 
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          are compatible and in fact equal. According to  Lemma 1 ,  
                            ≤ 
                          is an interval order iff  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          is a total quasi-order. According to  Lemma 2 ,  
                            ≤ 
                          is a semiorder provided that the intersection of  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  s 
                                  u 
                                  c 
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          is a total quasi-order. Hence, the order on an ordered group is an interval order iff this is a semiorder.

From this corollary, we are led to examine the order structure of groups equipped with a semiorder.

We start with an example. Ordering the intervals of the real numbers of the form  
                            [ 
                            a 
                            , 
                            a 
                            + 
                            1 
                            [ 
                          by the order defined in Eq.  (1)  of Section  2.4  amounts to order the reals by  
                            x 
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            y 
                          if  
                            x 
                            = 
                            y 
                          or  
                            1 
                            ≤ 
                            y 
                            − 
                            x 
                         . The additive group of the reals equipped with this order is an ordered group, the order is a semiorder, in fact a threshold order since  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                         
 coincides with the order  
                            ≤ 
                          on the reals. Replacing  
                            1 
                          by a positive real  
                            α 
                         , the ordering we obtain, say  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  α 
                               
                            
                         , is different, but the ordered set is isomorphic to the previous one.

This construction generalizes:

The  center  
                         
                            Z 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                          of a group  
                            G 
                          is the set of elements that commute with every element of  
                            G 
                         .

The proof is straightforward. Using the fact that  
                            α 
                            ∈ 
                            Z 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                         , one proves first that

                                  α 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                     
                                        ˇ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          are compatible order relations. Next, one proves that the quasi-orders  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤

                                  p 
                                  r

                          associated to  
                            
                               
                                  ≤

                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                     
                                        ˇ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          are equal to  
                            ⪯ 
                         . We will see below how to deduce this result from the general construction given in  Proposition 11 .

In the above examples,  
                            α 
                          is a  threshold ; to distinguish the first example from the second, we say that in the first example the threshold is  attained .

We prove the following extension of Scott–Suppes’ representation of semiorders.

We illustrate this result first with a new notion of embeddability between posets and the corresponding equivalence: two posets being equivalent if every group embedding one of these posets embeds the other (see Section  9  and Item (2) of  Proposition 44 ). Next, we illustrate it with the notion of dimension. Let  
                            P 
                            ≔ 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          be a poset. A  linear extension  of  
                            ≤ 
                          (or of  
                            P 
                         ) is a total order  
                            ⪯ 
                          on  
                            X 
                          such that  
                            x 
                            ⪯ 
                            y 
                          whenever  
                            x 
                            ≤ 
                            y 
                         
                         Szpilrajn (1930) . The  dimension  of  
                            P 
                         , denoted by  
                            d 
                            i 
                            m 
                            
                               ( 
                               P 
                               ) 
                            
                         , is the least cardinal  
                            κ 
                          such that there exists a family  
                            R 
                          of  
                            κ 
                          linear extensions  
                            
                               
                                  ⪯ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                         ,  
                            i 
                            < 
                            κ 
                         , of  
                            ≤ 
                          so that  
                            x 
                            ≤ 
                            y 
                          iff  
                            x 
                            
                               
                                  ⪯ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                            y 
                          for all  
                            i 
                            < 
                            κ 
                          ( Dushnik & Miller, 1941 ). The dimension of finite posets is finite, and the dimension of finite interval orders is unbounded (see  Bogart et al. (1976)  and  Füredi, Hajnal, Rödl, and Trotter (1991) ). On the other hand and according to Rabinovitch ( Rabinovitch, 1978 ), finite semiorders have dimension at most  
                            3 
                         . This extends to infinite semiorders (via the Compactness Theorem of first order logic). In Section  8  we give an effective proof that threshold groups, with attained threshold, have dimension at most  
                            3 
                         . Thus, Rabinovitch’s result follows from  Theorem 7  and  Proposition 43 .

Let us illustrate the scope of  Theorem 4 . Let  
                            n 
                          be a positive integer. For  
                            n 
                            = 
                            1 
                          or  
                            n 
                            = 
                            2 
                         ,  Theorem 4  says nothing. But, as it is immediate to see by using translations,  an ordered group 
                         
                            G 
                          
                         does not embed 
                         
                            1 
                            ⊕ 
                            n 
                          
                         if and only if the set  
                         
                            i 
                            n 
                            c 
                            
                               ( 
                               0 
                               ) 
                            
                          
                         of elements of 
                         
                            G 
                          
                         which are incomparable to  
                         
                            0 
                          
                         does not embed an 
                         
                            n 
                         
                         -element chain . For a poset, not embedding an  
                            n 
                         -element chain amounts to be a union of less than  
                            n 
                          antichains ( Mirsky, 1971 ); for  
                            n 
                            = 
                            3 
                         , these posets are said to be  bipartite . We are led to the following:

                                     0

Ordered groups which do not embed  
                            1 
                            ⊕ 
                            1 
                          are totally ordered groups. Ordered groups whose order is a weak order are easy to describe. Indeed, we prove in Section  5.2  that:

A  torsion  element of a group  
                            G 
                          is any element  
                            x 
                          such that  
                            n 
                            x 
                            = 
                            0 
                          for some positive integer  
                            n 
                         . We recall that if  
                            G 
                          is abelian, the set  
                            T 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                          of torsion elements is a subgroup and the quotient  
                            G 
                            ∕ 
                            T 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                          is torsion free and abelian hence totally orderable ( Levi, 1913 ). If  
                            T 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                          is nontrivial it follows from the following corollary that  
                            G 
                          can be equipped with a compatible weak order distinct from a total order. On the other hand, an abelian group need not have torsion elements to be equipped with a compatible weak order distinct from a total order. For instance let  
                            G 
                            ≔ 
                            Z 
                            × 
                            Z 
                          which is torsion free. Set  
                            
                               
                                  G 
                               
                               
                                  + 
                               
                            
                            ≔ 
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  , 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                               } 
                            
                            ∪ 
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ) 
                               
                               : 
                               x 
                               > 
                               0 
                               } 
                            
                         . This induces a weak order not a total order on  
                            G 
                         .

Corollary 9 
                            
                               A group can be equipped with a weak order distinct from the equality relation (hence with a nontrivial semiorder) iff it admits a totally ordered quotient by some proper subgroup.

A group can be equipped with a weak order distinct from the equality relation (hence with a nontrivial semiorder) iff it admits a totally ordered quotient by some proper subgroup.

A strengthening of this corollary is given in  Proposition 20 .

The case  
                            n 
                            = 
                            3 
                          of  Theorem 4  corresponds to ordered groups for which the order is a semiorder. We will describe the compatible semiorders and particularly the threshold orders on groups. In the vein of  Proposition 8 , we will prove in Section  5  the following result:

A threshold order is a weak order if and only if it is a total order. This particularly applies to ordered groups. Indeed,  
                            G 
                            ≔ 
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               + 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          is a threshold group and  
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          is a weak order if and only if  
                            ≤ 
                          is total (see  Proposition 8 ).

For  
                            n 
                            > 
                            3 
                         , a particular aspect of  Problem 1  is:  which posets necessarily embed into ordered groups 
                         
                            G 
                          
                         such that 
                         
                            i 
                            n 
                            c 
                            
                               ( 
                               0 
                               ) 
                            
                          
                         is the union of  
                         
                            n 
                            − 
                            1 
                          
                         antichains ?

This leads us to introduce in Section  9  a quasi-order on the class of posets which extends the embeddability relation and to describe some of the equivalence classes associated to this quasi-order.

In the next proposition we characterize compatible semiorders on groups and threshold groups in terms of an auxiliary total quasi-order and a final segment.

The fact that a group  
                            G 
                          admits a compatible total order imposes that  
                            G 
                          be torsion-free (hence, nontrivial subgroups are infinite). Since every abelian and torsion-free group can be totally ordered ( Levi, 1913 ) we infer that:

Corollary 12 
                            
                               Every torsion-free abelian group can be equipped with a compatible threshold order which is not total.

Every torsion-free abelian group can be equipped with a compatible threshold order which is not total.

Different total orders which are compatible with the group operation will lead to different semiorders. Compatible total orders on torsion free abelian groups and notably on groups of the form  
                            
                               
                                  Z 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          have been described (see Chapter 6 of  [23]  and  Robbiano (1985) ). The case of the additive group of real numbers is interesting for our purpose. According to  Proposition 11 , if  
                            ≤ 
                          is a semiorder such that  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  p 
                                  r 
                                  e 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                         
 coincides with the order  
                            ⪯ 
                          on the reals then  
                            ≤ 
                          is one of the orders  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  α 
                               
                            
                          or  
                            
                               
                                  ≤ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                     
                                        ˇ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          (for some real  
                            α 
                         ) defined in  Proposition 6 . Hence  
                            ≤ 
                          is a threshold order. But this does not imply that every semiorder on  
                            R 
                          has this form, because the semiorder depends upon the compatible total order on the reals and there are plenty of those (in fact there are  
                            
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          many, where  
                            c 
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ℵ 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         ). For example, viewing  
                            R 
                          as a vector space over the field  
                            Q 
                          of rational numbers, let  
                            H 
                          be a basis of  
                            R 
                          (the existence of such a basis, called a Hamel basis, requires some form of the axiom of choice) and for every real  
                            x 
                          let  
                            s 
                            u 
                            p 
                            p 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                         , the  support  of  
                            x 
                         , be the set of members of  
                            B 
                          which appear in a decomposition of  
                            x 
                          over  
                            Q 
                         . Put a total order  
                            ⪯ 
                          on  
                            H 
                          and extend it to a total order on the reals by defining the positive cone  
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  H 
                               
                            
                            ≔ 
                            
                               { 
                               0 
                               } 
                            
                            ∪ 
                            
                               { 
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               R 
                               ∖ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  0 
                                  } 
                               
                               : 
                               l 
                               e

                               d 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               > 
                               0 
                               } 
                            
                         , where  
                            l 
                            e 
                            a 
                            d 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                         , the  leading coefficient  of  
                            x 
                         , is the coefficient of the largest element of  
                            s 
                            u 
                            p 
                            p 
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               ) 
                            
                          which appears in the decomposition of  
                            x 
                         . According to  Proposition 11 , any final segment in the positive cone will lead to a semiorder and, in some instances, to a threshold order. Some continuity or density conditions on a compatible total order on the reals in order to recover the ordinary order are necessary. This explains somehow why the representation of infinite semiorders in the reals was a difficult problem ( Candeal et al., 2012; Candeal & Induráin, 2010 ).

There are  
                            
                               
                                  2 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ℵ 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          compatible total orders on the additive group  
                            
                               
                                  Z 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         . Here is an example of a threshold group on  
                            
                               
                                  Z 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         . The positive cone is the set  
                            C 
                            ≔ 
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  , 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                               } 
                            
                            ∪ 
                            
                               {

                                  n 
                                  , 
                                  m 
                                  ) 
                               
                               : 
                               n 
                               ≥

                               ≥

                               < 
                               0 
                               
                               and 
                               
                               m 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                               } 
                            
                         . The idea is the same as above: set  
                            B 
                            ≔ 
                            
                               {

                                  1

                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ,

                                  0

                          then every element of

                          is a combination of members of  
                            B

                               ( 
                               1

                               0 
                               ) 
                            
                            ≺

                               0

For nonabelian groups this conclusion of  Corollary 12  is no longer valid. In  Clifford (1951) , Clifford exhibits an example of a nonabelian and nonfinitely generated totally ordered order-simple group (an ordered group is  order-simple  if every normal and convex subgroup is trivial). We prove that in Clifford’s example the set of positive elements contains no proper normal final segment (see  Proposition 40  in Section  7 ). Hence, the only compatible semiorder on this group is either the total order or the equality relation.

According to  
                            
                               ( 
                               b 
                               ) 
                            
                          of  Proposition 11 , if  
                            G 
                          is a threshold group obtained via a total order  
                            ⪯ 
                          and a nonempty normal final segment  
                            F 
                          of  
                            
                               { 
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               G 
                               : 
                               0 
                               ≺ 
                               x 
                               } 
                            
                         , the set  
                            I 
                            ≔ 
                            G 
                            ∖ 
                            
                               ( 
                               − 
                               F 
                               ∪ 
                               F 
                               ) 
                            
                          cannot be a subgroup distinct from  
                            
                               { 
                               0 
                               } 
                            
                         .

In Section  7.1 , we prove:

Theorem 13 
                            
                               Every finitely generated totally ordered group  
                               
                                  G 
                                
                               admits a proper normal final segment  
                               
                                  F 
                                
                               such that  
                               
                                  I 
                                  ≔ 
                                  G 
                                  ∖ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     − 
                                     F 
                                     ∪ 
                                     F 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               is not a group.

Every finitely generated totally ordered group  
                               
                                  G 
                                
                               admits a proper normal final segment  
                               
                                  F 
                                
                               such that  
                               
                                  I 
                                  ≔ 
                                  G 
                                  ∖ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     − 
                                     F 
                                     ∪ 
                                     F 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               is not a group.

Two widely studied groups in Group Theory are the Thompson group  
                            F 
                          and the free group  
                            F 
                            
                               ( 
                               S 
                               ) 
                            
                          on a set  
                            S 
                          of generators.

The group  
                            F 
                          was introduced by Richard Thompson in some unpublished handwritten notes in 1965 ( Thompson ) as a possible counterexample to von Neumann conjecture. The group  
                            F 
                          has a collection of unusual properties which have made it a counterexample to many general conjectures in group theory. The group  
                            F 
                          is not simple but its derived subgroup  
                            
                               [ 
                               F 
                               , 
                               F 
                               ] 
                            
                          is and the quotient of  
                            F 
                          by its derived subgroup is the free abelian group of rank 2. The Thompson group can be generated by two elements and can be equipped with a compatible total order ( Cannon, Floyd, & Parry, 1996; Navas & Rivas, 2010 ).

The free group  
                            F 
                            
                               ( 
                               S 
                               ) 
                            
                          is a universal group: Every group is isomorphic to a quotient group of some free group. The free group with finitely many generators can also be equipped with a compatible total order ( Magnus, Karrass, & Solitar, 1976 ).

A consequence of  Theorem 13  is this.

Corollary 14 
                            
                               The Thompson group  
                               
                                  F 
                                
                               and the free group on finitely many generators can be equipped with a compatible semiorder which is not a weak order.

The Thompson group  
                               
                                  F 
                                
                               and the free group on finitely many generators can be equipped with a compatible semiorder which is not a weak order.

Problem 2 
                            Is it true that on every finitely generated totally ordered group there is a compatible threshold order which is not total?

Is it true that on every finitely generated totally ordered group there is a compatible threshold order which is not total?
