Example 6 
                            Let  
                                  K 
                                  , 
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  B 
                                  , 
                                  C 
                                be four groups. We suppose that  
                                  B 
                                is an additive subgroup of the reals, ordered with an attained threshold  
                                  α 
                               , as in  Example 2 . We suppose that  
                                  A 
                                and  
                                  C 
                                are totally ordered groups and that  
                                  F 
                                is a nonempty normal final segment of  
                                  A 
                                which is not a union of cosets of some nontrivial convex subgroup of  
                                  A 
                               . We suppose that  
                                  K 
                                is ordered with the equality relation. Let  
                                  G 
                                  ≔ 
                                  K 
                                  × 
                                  A 
                                  × 
                                  B 
                                  × 
                                  C 
                                be the direct product of the groups  
                                  K 
                                  , 
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  B 
                                and  
                                  C 
                               . We define a compatible semiorder on  
                                  G 
                               . For that we order  
                                  B 
                                  × 
                                  C 
                                as the lexicographical sum over  
                                  C 
                                of copies of the order on  
                                  B 
                               . We order the product  
                                  A 
                                  × 
                                  B 
                                as  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ≔ 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        ⊙ 
                                     
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                  
                                  B 
                               , as in  Example 5 . Finally, we order  
                                  K 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  × 
                                  C 
                                as the lexicographical sum over  
                                  C 
                                of copies of the order on  
                                  K 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′

                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  × 
                                  C 
                                is a threshold group and  
                                  G 
                                is a semiordered group.

Let  
                                  K 
                                  , 
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  B 
                                  , 
                                  C 
                                be four groups. We suppose that  
                                  B 
                                is an additive subgroup of the reals, ordered with an attained threshold  
                                  α

                                  A

                                  C

                                  F 
                                is a nonempty normal final segment of  
                                  A 
                                which is not a union of cosets of some nontrivial convex subgroup of  
                                  A

                                  K

                                  G 
                                  ≔ 
                                  K 
                                  × 
                                  A 
                                  × 
                                  B 
                                  × 
                                  C

                                  K 
                                  , 
                                  A

                                  B

                                  G 
                               . For that we order  
                                  B 
                                  × 
                                  C

                                  C 
                                of copies of the order on  
                                  B 
                               . We order the product  
                                  A 
                                  × 
                                  B

                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′

                                        F 
                                     
                                  
                                  B

                                  K 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′

                                  C

                                  K 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     
                                        A

                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                  × 
                                  C 
                                is a threshold group and  
                                  G 
                                is a semiordered group.

Proof 
                            The order on  
                                  G 
                                is the lexicographical sum over  
                                  C 
                                of copies of the order on  
                                  K 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′

                                  G

                                  K 
                                  ×

                                  K

                                  K 
                                and

                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                is a semiorder, we infer that the order on  
                                  K 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                is a semiorder. This proves that  
                                  G

                                        A

                                is a threshold group (see  Example 5 ). □

In this subsection, we prove that the examples given above describe the possible semiorders on groups, e.g.  Theorem 17  shows that the order on a group is a semiorder iff it can be expressed as a product like in  Example 6 . Of course, the group does not need to decompose as in this example (this depends upon the existence of direct factors for the subgroups).

The following result gives insights on the structure of convex subgroups of an ordered group whose order is a semiorder.

Theorem 19 
                            
                               Let  
                               
                                  G 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     + 
                                     , 
                                     ≤ 
                                     )

                                  ≤ 
                                
                               is a semiorder. If  
                               
                                  H

                                  G

                                     (

                                     G 
                                     )

                                     0

                                  H

                                  G 
                                  ∕ 
                                  H 
                                
                               is totally ordered and 
                               
                                  G 
                                
                               is the lexicographical sum of the cosets of  
                               
                                  H 
                               
                               .

Let

A consequence of  Theorems 15 and 17  is the following proposition.

Proposition 20 
                            
                               For a group  
                               
                                  G 
                                
                               the following properties are equivalent: 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     (i) 
                                     
                                        
                                           G 
                                         
                                        can be endowed with a compatible semiorder distinct from the equality relation; 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (ii) 
                                     
                                        
                                           G 
                                         
                                        can be endowed with a compatible weak order distinct from the equality relation; 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (iii) 
                                     
                                        
                                           G 
                                         
                                        has a proper normal subgroup  
                                        
                                           H 
                                         
                                        whose quotient  
                                        
                                           G 
                                           ∕ 
                                           H 
                                         
                                        is totally orderable.

For a group  
                               
                                  G 
                                
                               the following properties are equivalent:

                                           H 
                                         
                                        whose quotient

                                           ∕ 
                                           H 
                                         
                                        is totally orderable.

This paper contains six more sections. Section  4  contains some general properties of convex sets and autonomous sets of a quasi-ordered group. Section  5  contains properties of normal subsets of a group and the proofs of  Propositions 8 ,  11  and  Theorem 10 . Section  6  describes some properties of the groups  
                            I 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               0 
                               ) 
                            
                          and  
                            A 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                          and contains the proofs of  Theorem 15  to  19  about  
                            K 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                         ,  
                            I 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               0 
                               ) 
                            
                          and  
                            A 
                            
                               ( 
                               G 
                               ) 
                            
                         . Section  7  contains a description of Clifford’s example and a proof of  Theorem 13 . Section  8  contains an effective proof that a threshold group with attained threshold has dimension at most three. Section  9  introduces an extension of the quasi-order of embeddability between posets and describes some equivalence classes.

Let  
                         G 
                       be a group,  
                         H 
                       be a normal subgroup of  
                         G 
                       and  
                         G 
                         ∕ 
                         H 
                       be the quotient group. Suppose that  
                         G 
                       is equipped with a compatible quasi-order  
                         ≤ 
                      . Let  
                         
                            
                               ≤ 
                            
                            
                               H 
                            
                         
                       be the image of  
                         ≤ 
                       by the quotient map. That is  
                         α 
                         
                            
                               ≤ 
                            
                            
                               H 
                            
                         
                         β 
                       in  
                         G 
                         ∕ 
                         H 
                       if  
                         a 
                         ≤ 
                         b

                         a 
                         ∈ 
                         α 
                       and  
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         β 
                       (equivalently,  
                         a 
                         ≤ 
                         b 
                       for every  
                         a 
                         ∈ 
                         α 
                       and some  
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         β 
                      ). The relation  
                         
                            
                               ≤ 
                            
                            
                               H 
                            
                         
                       is a compatible quasi-order (Proposition 4, page 25 of  Kopytov and Medvedev (1994) ). Furthermore:

Lemma 22

Proof

                                     ≤

                            . Hence,  
                               H 
                             is convex. For the converse, note first that from (a) of  Lemma 3 ,  
                               
                                  
                                     0

                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               H 
                            ). More generally, let

                             is isomorphic to the lexicographical sum of

                             indexed by

                               ∕

                            . 
                         Next, we prove that the quasi-order

Remark.  Note that in general

                         ≔ 
                         Z 
                         ×

                         ∕ 
                         2

                         Z

                         2

                         H 
                         ≔ 
                         Z 
                         × 
                         
                            { 
                            0

                               H 
                            
                         
                       is the equality relation on

                         =

                       is the equality relation on the quotient.

A set of subsets that forms a totally ordered set under inclusion will be called a  chain  of subsets. For the following two lemmas we refer to  Glass (1999)  Chapter 3 (Lemmas 3.1.1 and 3.1.2).

Lemma 23 
                         
                            If  
                            
                               G 
                             
                            is a partially ordered group and  
                            
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  : 
                                  i 
                                  ∈ 
                                  I 
                                  } 
                               
                             
                            is a family of convex subgroups of  
                            
                               G 
                            
                            , then  
                            
                               ∩ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  : 
                                  i 
                                  ∈ 
                                  I 
                                  } 
                               
                             
                            is a convex subgroup of G. If  
                            
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  : 
                                  i 
                                  ∈ 
                                  I 
                                  } 
                               
                             
                            is a chain of convex subgroups of  
                            
                               G 
                            
                            , then  
                            
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  : 
                                  i 
                                  ∈ 
                                  I 
                                  } 
                               
                             
                            is a convex subgroup of  
                            
                               G 
                            
                            .

Let  
                         G 
                       be a partially ordered group and  
                         g 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                         ∖ 
                         
                            { 
                            0 
                            } 
                         
                      . Let  
                         V 
                       be the set of all convex subgroups  
                         V 
                       of  
                         G 
                       such that  
                         g 
                         ⁄ 
                         ∈ 
                         V 
                      . By  Lemma 23  the union of any chain of subsets in  
                         V 
                       is itself in  
                         V 
                      . Since  
                         
                            { 
                            0 
                            } 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         V 
                      , we have maximal elements in  
                         V 
                       by Zorn’s Lemma. Hence for each  
                         g 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                         ∖ 
                         
                            { 
                            0 
                            } 
                         
                      , there is a convex subgroup  
                         
                            
                               C 
                            
                            
                               g 
                            
                         
                       maximal not containing  
                         g 
                      ; i.e.: the union of all convex subgroups not containing  
                         g 
                      . If  
                         G 
                       is totally ordered, Zorn’s Lemma is not required.

Lemma 24 
                         
                            If  
                            
                               G 
                             
                            is a totally ordered group, the set of all convex subgroups of 
                            
                               G 
                             
                            is a complete chain. Hence, for every  
                            
                               g 
                               ∈ 
                               G 
                               ∖ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  0 
                                  } 
                               
                             
                            there is a largest convex subgroup  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                                  
                                     g 
                                  
                               
                             
                            of  
                            
                               G 
                             
                            which does not contain  
                            
                               g 
                            
                            .

If  
                            
                               G 
                             
                            is a totally ordered group, the set of all convex subgroups of 
                            
                               G 
                             
                            is a complete chain. Hence, for every  
                            
                               g 
                               ∈ 
                               G 
                               ∖ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  0 
                                  } 
                               
                             
                            there is a largest convex subgroup  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                                  
                                     g 
                                  
                               
                             
                            of

                               g

Let us recall that an ordered group is  archimedean  if for all  
                         x 
                         , 
                         y 
                         > 
                         0 
                       there is a nonnegative integer  
                         n 
                       such that  
                         n 
                         x 
                         ≰ 
                         y 
                      . Note that for a totally ordered group this means  
                         n 
                         x 
                         > 
                         y 
                      . The following characterization of totally ordered archimedean groups is known as Hölder’s theorem ( Hölder, 1901 ). For completeness, we indicate the scheme of the proof.

Theorem 25 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               G 
                             
                            be a totally ordered group. The following propositions are equivalent. 
                         
                         
                            
                               
                                  (i) 
                                  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     is archimedean. 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  (ii) 
                                  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     is isomorphic as an ordered group to a subgroup of the additive group of the real numbers (in particular,  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     is abelian). 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  (iii) 
                                  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     has no nontrivial convex subgroups.

Let  
                            
                               G 
                             
                            be a totally ordered group. The following propositions are equivalent.

(i) 
                                  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     is archimedean. 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  (ii) 
                                  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     is isomorphic as an ordered group to a subgroup of the additive group of the real numbers (in particular,  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     is abelian). 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  (iii) 
                                  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     has no nontrivial convex subgroups.

G 
                                      
                                     is archimedean.

G 
                                      
                                     is isomorphic as an ordered group to a subgroup of the additive group of the real numbers (in particular,  
                                     
                                        G 
                                      
                                     is abelian).

G 
                                      
                                     has no nontrivial convex subgroups.

Proof 
                         The implication  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ⇒ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is due to Hölder, see  Glass (1999)  Theorem 4.a, page 56. 
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ⇒ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                             It is well known that every subgroup of  
                               R 
                             is either discrete, that is it is of the form  
                               x 
                               Z 
                             for some  
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               R 
                            , or is dense in  
                               R 
                            . Hence a nontrivial subgroup of  
                               R 
                             cannot be convex. 
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ⇒ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  )

                             It is well known that every subgroup of

Proof of Fact 1 
                            
                               Fact 1  is a well known, elementary fact of beginning group theory. We do not include its proof.  □

Fact 1  is a well known, elementary fact of beginning group theory. We do not include its proof.  □

                                           p 
                                           r 
                                           e 
                                           d 
                                        
                                     
                                   we get a contradiction too.

Lemma 26 
                            
                               Let  
                               
                                  ⪯ 
                                
                               be a compatible total quasi-order on a group  
                               
                                  G 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     + 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               and let  
                               
                                  F 
                                
                               be a final segment of  
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     ⪯ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                
                               not containing  
                               
                                  0 
                               
                               . Define a binary relation  
                               
                                  ≤ 
                                
                               on  
                               
                                  G 
                                
                               as follows:  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                        ≤ 
                                        y 
                                         if  
                                        x 
                                        = 
                                        y 
                                         or  
                                        y 
                                        − 
                                        x 
                                        ∈ 
                                        F 
                                        .

                                     (a) 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≤ 
                                         
                                        is an order. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (b) 
                                     
                                        
                                           ⪯ 
                                         
                                        is an extension of  
                                        
                                           ≤ 
                                         
                                        and both  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ≤ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 p 
                                                 r 
                                                 e 
                                                 d

                                                 s 
                                                 u 
                                                 c 
                                                 c 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        extend  
                                        
                                           ⪯ 
                                        
                                        . 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (c) 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≤ 
                                         
                                        is a semiorder. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (d) 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≤ 
                                         
                                        is compatible iff  
                                        
                                           F 
                                         
                                        is normal.

Let  
                               
                                  ⪯ 
                                
                               be a compatible total quasi-order on a group  
                               
                                  G 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     + 
                                     )

                                     ,

                               not containing  
                               
                                  0 
                               
                               . Define a binary relation  
                               
                                  ≤ 
                                
                               on  
                               
                                  G 
                                
                               as follows:  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                        ≤ 
                                        y 
                                         if  
                                        x 
                                        = 
                                        y 
                                         or  
                                        y 
                                        − 
                                        x 
                                        ∈ 
                                        F 
                                        .

(a) 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≤ 
                                         
                                        is an order. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (b) 
                                     
                                        
                                           ⪯ 
                                         
                                        is an extension of  
                                        
                                           ≤ 
                                         
                                        and both  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ≤ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 p

                                                 e

                                                 c

                                        .

                                        is a semiorder.

                                        is compatible iff  
                                        
                                           F 
                                         
                                        is normal.

≤ 
                                         
                                        is an order.

⪯ 
                                         
                                        is an extension of  
                                        
                                           ≤ 
                                         
                                        and both  
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 ≤ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 p 
                                                 r

                                                 c 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        extend  
                                        
                                           ⪯ 
                                        
                                        .

≤ 
                                         
                                        is a semiorder.

≤ 
                                         
                                        is compatible iff  
                                        
                                           F 
                                         
                                        is normal.

Proof 
                            We observe that since  
                                  ⪯ 
                                is total,  
                                  F 
                                is a final segment of  
                                  
                                     
                                        G 
                                     
                                     
                                        + 
                                     
                                  
                                  ≔ 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     x 
                                     ∈ 
                                     X 
                                     : 
                                     0 
                                     ⪯ 
                                     x 
                                     } 
                                  
                                equipped with the compatible quasi-order  
                                  ⪯ 
                               . Hence,  
                                  F 
                                is a cone, i.e.  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                                implies  
                                  x 
                                  + 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                               . Indeed, if  
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                                and  
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                                then since  
                                  0 
                                  ⪯ 
                                  y 
                                and  
                                  ⪯ 
                                is a compatible quasi-order, we have  
                                  x 
                                  ⪯ 
                                  x 
                                  + 
                                  y 
                               . Since  
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                                and  
                                  F 
                                is a final segment  
                                  x 
                                  + 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                               . 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                The fact that  
                                  ≤ 
                                is reflexive follows from the definition. Next,  
                                  ≤ 
                                is transitive. Indeed, let  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  , 
                                  z 
                                be such that  
                                  x 
                                  ≤ 
                                  y 
                                  ≤ 
                                  z 
                               . If  
                                  x 
                                  = 
                                  y 
                                or  
                                  y 
                                  = 
                                  z 
                                then  
                                  x 
                                  ≤ 
                                  z 
                               . If not, then  
                                  y 
                                  − 
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                                and  
                                  z 
                                  − 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                               . Since  
                                  F 
                                is a cone,  
                                  z 
                                  − 
                                  x 
                                  = 
                                  z 
                                  − 
                                  y 
                                  + 
                                  y 
                                  − 
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                                hence  
                                  x 
                                  ≤ 
                                  z 
                               . Finally,  
                                  ≤ 
                                is antisymmetric, indeed if  
                                  y 
                                  − 
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                                and  
                                  x 
                                  − 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                               , then since  
                                  F 
                                is a cone  
                                  0 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F 
                                which is impossible. 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     b 
                                     )

                                  y

                                  x 
                                  =

                                  x

                                  y

                                  <

                                  y 
                                  −

                                  ≤

                                        p

Lemma 27 
                            
                               Let

The decomposition of the incomparability graph of a poset into connected components is expressed in the following lemma which belongs to the folklore of the theory of ordered sets.

Lemma 30 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               G 
                               =

                                  ,

                                  )

                                  G 
                                  )

                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a convex normal subgroup and  
                            
                               G 
                               ∕ 
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is totally ordered.

Let  
                            
                               G 
                               = 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  + 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            be an ordered group. Then  
                            
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a convex normal subgroup and  
                            
                               G 
                               ∕ 
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is totally ordered.

Proof 
                         According to  Lemma 28 , the poset  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is the lexicographical sum of its connected components. The order relation being compatible with the group operation, its complement is compatible, hence the transitive closure of this complement, that is the connectedness relation  
                               
                                  
                                     ∥ 
                                  
                                  ¯ 
                               
                             is an equivalence relation compatible with the group operation on  
                               G 
                            , hence the equivalence class of  
                               0 
                            , that is  
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                            , is a normal subgroup and  
                               G 
                               ∕ 
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is totally ordered.  □

                            of an ordered group  
                            
                               G 
                               =

                                  ,

Proof 
                         We prove that every  
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             belongs to  
                               H 
                             by induction of  
                               
                                  
                                     d 
                                  
                                  
                                     I 
                                     n 
                                     c 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        G 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ,

                                  ) 
                               
                            . The case  
                               n 
                               ≤ 
                               1

                               n 
                               > 
                               1 
                            . Then there is some  
                               y

                               = 
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            . Via the induction hypothesis,  
                               y 
                               ∈ 
                               H 
                            ; since  
                               y 
                             is incomparable to  
                               x

                               − 
                               y 
                               + 
                               x 
                             is incomparable to  
                               0 
                            , hence belongs to  
                               H

                               x 
                               = 
                               y 
                               + 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  − 
                                  y 
                                  + 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ∈ 
                               
                               H 
                            .  □

                               x

                               = 
                               n 
                               −

                               y

                               −

Corollary 36 
                         
                            If a threshold group  
                            
                               G 
                             
                            is not totally ordered and has no infinite antichain then 
                            
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is the group of integers equipped with a threshold order.

If a threshold group  
                            
                               G 
                             
                            is not totally ordered and has no infinite antichain then 
                            
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is the group of integers equipped with a threshold order.

Proof 
                         Assume  
                               G 
                             is a threshold group (so that  
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                     r 
                                     e 
                                     d 
                                  
                               
                             is a total order) with no infinite antichain. Since  
                               i 
                               n 
                               c 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is the union of (at most) two antichains, it must be finite. Since  
                               i 
                               n 
                               c 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  0 
                                  } 
                               
                             is an interval of  
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                     r 
                                     e 
                                     d 
                                  
                               
                             (cf. Item (4) of Section  5.1 ) and this interval is not reduced to  
                               0 
                            , we infer that  
                               0 
                             has a successor  
                               a 
                             in  
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                     r 
                                     e 
                                     d 
                                  
                               
                             and  
                               a 
                               ∈ 
                               i 
                               n 
                               c 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                            . Let  
                               b 
                             be the largest element of  
                               i 
                               n 
                               c 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                             (with respect to  
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                     r 
                                     e 
                                     d 
                                  
                               
                            ). Then  
                               α 
                               = 
                               a 
                               + 
                               b 
                             is the threshold of  
                               G 
                            . It follows from  Lemma 35  that  
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  G 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               Z 
                               a 
                            .  □

                               G

                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                     r

                               i

In this section we exhibit an example, due to Clifford, of an ordered group with the property that the set of positive elements contains no proper normal final segment.

Following Clifford ( Clifford, 1951 ), let  
                         G 
                       be the group generated by a set of symbols
